feed.title: Thinkers Dilemma

feed.description: Thinkers Dilemma is an introspective into the human existence.

items.title: The DEI Rollback and the Illusion it Shattered
items.author: Thinkers Dilemma
items.description: The Fallacy of Freedom and Equality
items.content:
Sometime in the quiet transition between the political volatility of 2024 and the corporate realignment of 2025, a series of coordinated, low-frequency signals began to emerge across the Fortune 500. It started not with a bang, but with a “simplified talent strategy” memo. The subject lines were sanitized. The word “diversity” was systematically scrubbed from mission statements, replaced by “meritocracy,” “inclusion,” or simply omitted altogether.

Within weeks, the Chief Diversity Officers who had been the high-profile faces of the post 2020 era found their roles being “realigned” into Human Resources. The Employee Resource Groups (ERGs) went quiet, their budgets frozen. And the people those programs had been built around, the ones recruited under the banner of inclusion learned, in a single forwarded memo, exactly what their “belonging” had been worth.

The rollback did not destroy something real. It revealed what was always compliance theater.

The dissolution of the DEI (Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion) apparatus is not merely a political retreat; it is a structural revelation. It has exposed the gap between the performance of equity and the practice of equity. And while the shattering of these programs is painful, the revelation may be more useful than the programs ever were.

THE ANATOMY OF THE DISAGGREGATION

To understand the rollback, one must first perform an analytical disaggregation. The term “DEI” has become a linguistic monolith, a “catch-all” that has been treated as a single entity by activists, litigators, and executives. But DEI was never one thing. It was two entirely different sets of corporate behavior masquerading as a single movement.

The first set was Genuine Equity Work. This was the unglamorous, technical, and highly effective work of structural reform. It consisted of pay band audits that uncovered six figure gaps between men and women performing identical roles. The structured interview processes designed to mitigate the “gut-feel” bias that favors candidates with similar social backgrounds to the interviewer; and sponsorship programs that connected junior employees of color with senior leaders who held actual decision-making power over headcount and compensation. This work was often invisible because it was embedded in the plumbing of the company. When it worked, it changed outcomes without requiring a press release.

The second set was the Performance Regime. This was the highly visible, highly marketable, and highly fragile “theater” of inclusion. It was the heritage month emails drafted by marketing departments; the unconscious bias training modules that satisfied legal exposure without changing a single hiring metric; and the Employee Resource Groups that substituted community for power.

These groups often provided marginalized employees with a Slack channel and a budget for lunch, but they were rarely given a seat at the table where compensation structures or promotion criteria were decided.

For years, most companies ran both simultaneously. They implemented the structural work (the plumbing) while simultaneously investing heavily in the performance (the theater). However, the performance regime created a fatal vulnerability. By allowing the most visible, most superficial version of the work to become the public face of the company’s values, corporations pre-wrote their own obituaries. When the political wind shifted, critics did not attack the pay audits or the structured interviews; they attacked the heritage-month emails and the “woke” slogans. Because the theater had become the public identity, the collapse of the theater necessitated the collapse of the plumbing.

THE POLITICAL AND LEGAL PINCERS

The dismantling of DEI was not an organic, bottom-up movement of exhausted employees. It was a coordinated, well funded pincer movement executed through legal, political, and reputational channels.

The catalyst was the 2023 Supreme Court decision in Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard. While the ruling technically applied to race conscious admissions in higher education, the legal downstream effects were immediate and profound. Conservative legal organizations and state attorneys general quickly identified a new weapon: Section 1981 of the Civil Rights Act of 1866. They argued that any corporate program specifically targeting racial groups, even those intended to increase representation could be interpreted as “racial preference” or “discrimination” against non-minority groups.

This created a climate of “litigation fear.” When a Fortune 500 company announces a diversity goal, they are no longer just stating a social preference; in the eyes of a modern activist litigator, they are creating “discoverable evidence” of a potential civil rights violation.

The corporate dominoes fell with startling speed. Following the legal templates laid out by conservative legal movements, we saw a pattern of strategic retreats.

It began with the removal of specific terminology. Companies moved from “Diversity” to “Inclusion” to “Opportunity” or “Global Belonging.” Then came the budgetary cuts. We saw major players like Tractor Supply and John Deere pivot away from “cultural awareness” initiatives. The pressure was not just legal; it was reputational, driven by high visibility social media activists like Robby Starbuck, who organized consumer boycotts against firms deemed “woke.”

The asymmetry of this movement is its most striking feature. Building inclusion is a slow, iterative, and often boring process of changing hiring pipelines and auditing pay. Dismantling it takes a single memo from a General Counsel.

Anything that can be created by executive fiat can be destroyed by executive fiat.

The programs that lived in a “title” or a “pledge” were the easiest to delete, because they were the ones most exposed to the light.

THE DATA GAP: WHY TRAINING FAILED

The credibility of any “rebuild” of DEI depends on a brutal assessment of what actually works. For years, the primary defense of DEI programs was the implementation of Unconscious Bias Training (UBT). As the rollback gains momentum, the data suggests that companies may have been defending the wrong thing.

The evidence on UBT is, at best, mixed. Extensive meta-analyses including significant work by Paluck et al. (2021) have found that while single session bias training can temporarily shift attitudes, it rarely translates into sustained behavioral change. In many cases, it can even trigger “backlash effects,” where participants who feel coerced by the training develop a renewed resentment toward the very groups the training intended to support.

The industry’s “gold standard” for DEI was actually its weakest tool. The companies that most aggressively marketed their “training modules” were the ones least likely to see a change in their actual demographic data.

Conversely, the data on structural interventions is overwhelming. Structured interviews where every candidate is asked the same set of predetermined questions and scored on a rubric significantly reduce bias compared to “conversational” interviews. Sponsorship, the act of a senior leader using their actual political capital to advocate for a junior employee, outperforms mentorship by a massive margin because it involves the exercise of power, not just the offering of advice.

The companies that made the most significant progress in representation between 2020 and 2024 were not those with the most colorful heritage month calendars; they were the companies that tied executive compensation to measurable, structural retention and promotion metrics. When those incentives are removed, as they have been at several major US retailers and financial institutions, the structural progress stalls. The data proves that you cannot “train” your way into equity; you can only “systematize” your way there.

THE RESISTANCE: GOVERNANCE VS. POSTURE

Amidst the mass exodus of DEI departments, a small group of “holdouts” has emerged. These are not necessarily “progressive” companies in the ideological sense; rather, they are institutions with the governance structure to treat inclusion as a business necessity rather than a political posture.

The pattern among the holdouts, companies like Costco, Apple, and Levi’s is telling. When faced with anti DEI shareholder proposals, these firms did not defend their programs on moralistic or political grounds. Instead, they defended them on the grounds of governance and talent acquisition. Their argument is pragmatic: in a globalized economy, the ability to attract and retain a diverse workforce is a fundamental business capability.

The conflict is ultimately between two views of the corporation. One view sees the corporation as a political actor, susceptible to the whims of the current cultural zeitgeist. The other view sees the corporation as a machine for talent optimization, where any barrier to the movement of the best talent, whether that barrier is gender, race, or disability, is a failure of efficiency.

The companies that “held the line” did so by making inclusion a part of the operational core rather than a separate, peripheral department. They realized that if you make “Diversity” a standalone department, you make it a target. If you make “Inclusion” a part of your standard operating procedure for hiring and compensation, it becomes much harder to excise.

THE PSYCHOLOGICAL COST: BELONGING VS. COMPLIANCE

There is a human cost to this era of rollback that does not show up on a balance sheet. For the millions of employees who entered the workforce during the 2020 – 2022 period, the “DEI era” provided a sense of institutional belonging. They were told that the company valued their perspective and was actively working to ensure their advancement.

The rollback has revealed that for many, this was not belonging; it was conditional inclusion.

t was a tenancy that could be revoked the moment the political weather changed or the legal risk profile shifted. This creates a profound psychological “decoupling.” When a company’s public-facing values are stripped away in the face of pressure, the employees who were promised those values do not simply “get over it.” They realize that the contract was illusory.

The result is a shift in how marginalized employees organize. The DEI era taught employees to look upward—to HR, to the Chief Diversity Officer, to the CEO—for protection and advancement. The post-DEI era is teaching them to look sideways. If the institution cannot be trusted to maintain its own stated values, employees will turn to peer networks, external professional organizations, and collective action to secure the power that the corporate hierarchy has withdrawn.

THE QUIET REVOLUTION: WHAT COMES NEXT

We are currently witnessing the end of “Performative DEI,” but we are not witnessing the end of the work.

The next phase of inclusion will be quieter, more technical, and much harder to attack. It will not live in a specialized department that can be “downsized” to save costs. Instead, it will be embedded into the standard infrastructure of human capital management.

We will see it in:

Pay Transparency: Legislation is already forcing companies to reveal salary bands, making the “equity” part of DEI a matter of public record rather than internal audit.

Algorithmic Auditing: As hiring becomes increasingly automated, the “equity” work will shift toward ensuring that the code itself is not replicating historical biases.

Procurement and Design: Inclusion will be treated as a “design constraint” in product development and a “standard requirement” in supply chain management.

The renaming wave, the shift to “Global Inclusion” or “Social Impact” or “Talent Opportunity”is a signal of this transition. Some of it is a defensive crouch, a way for companies to keep the same work while changing the name to avoid being a target. But some of it is a strategic evolution.

The era of the “DEI Executive” as a public-facing symbol of corporate morality is dying. In its place, a more robust, less visible, and more structural approach is emerging. The rollback has ended the theater. The real work: the slow, unglamorous, and highly technical work of changing how humans are valued within an organization, is only just beginning. The opportunity now is to build a version of inclusion that cannot be undone by a memo, because it was never written in one.

Thanks for reading! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Available for iOS and Android

Verified Sources

Students for Fair Admissions, Inc. v. President and Fellows of Harvard College, 600 U.S. 181 (2023). [Supreme Court Ruling on Race-Conscious Admissions]

Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1866 (Section 1981): Regarding the legality of race-conscious contracts and employment practices.

Academic Research & Meta-Analyses

Forscher, C. S., et al. (2019). “Reducing Unconscious Biases: A Meta-Analysis of Behavior Change.” Journal of Personality and Social Psychology.

Paluck, E. L., et al. (2017/2021). “Prejudice Reduction: Progress and Challenges in Social Science Research.” Annual Review of Psychology.

Business & Economic Analysis

McKinsey & Company. (2020/2023). Diversity Wins: How Inclusion Matters. [Report on the correlation between diversity and corporate profitability].

The Conference Board. (2024/2025). Trends in Shareholder Activism and DEI Proposals.

Media & Industry Reports

Axios. (2024/2025). The Corporate Retreat: A Survey of DEI Rollbacks in the Fortune 500.

Fortune. (2024/2025). The New Risk Profile: How Litigation is Reshaping Corporate Social Responsibility.

CNN Business. (2025). Governance and the Holdouts: Why Institutional Investors are Protecting DEI.

items.title: Diaspora Dispatch: The American Dream moves Abroad
items.author: Thinkers Dilemma
items.description: Leaving America: What It Means When the Dream Moves
items.content:
The Second Leaving

My family came to this country with a specific understanding. It was not a contract, no one handed them a document. It was closer to a mythology, passed through communities like a frequency you could tune into if you paid close enough attention.

Work hard. Stay quiet. Achieve.

In exchange, you would be permitted to build something here. Not equality…nobody promised that. Not belonging, that was never part of the offer. Permission was. Permission to work, to accumulate, to send your children into the professional class, to become, over time, an acceptable version of “American”.

That mythology is not dead. But something has shifted in the last two years that has made a growing number of people, not just immigrants and their children, but native-born Americans across demographics question whether the terms of the exchange have changed unilaterally. Whether the offer is still on the table. Whether the table itself is still standing.

For the diaspora professional community, people who came to America, or whose parents came to America, and who built professional lives, raised children, paid taxes, and invested decades into the premise of this country - that question carries a particular weight. Because leaving would not just be emigration.

It would be a second displacement. And for many of us, the first one was not optional.

“The question is not whether leaving America is reasonable. The data shows it is happening at a scale not recorded since 1935. The question is what it means — and what it signals about the country being left.”

The numbers behind the feeling

Before this becomes a personal essay, it is worth establishing that what we are describing is not a mood or a media narrative. It is a documented demographic shift.

Key data (2024–2026): 5.5 million US citizens living abroad as of October 2024. In 2025, the US recorded its first net negative migration since 1935. Approximately 150,000 more people left than arrived. Citizenship renunciations reached 4,820 in 2024 (a 48% increase from the prior year), then surged 102% in Q1 2025 alone. The queue for renunciation appointments now exceeds 30,000 people. The full-year 2025 figure is projected to exceed 6,000, an all-time record.

The desire to leave has been climbing steadily across administrations.

Under Bush and Obama, roughly 10 to 11 percent of Americans expressed a desire to emigrate. That figure rose to 16 percent during the first Trump administration. By November 2025, one in five Americans overall expressed a desire to leave. Among women aged 15 to 44, that number reached 40 percent. That’s up from 10 percent just over a decade ago, and four times higher than any other age and gender cohort.

After the 2024 presidential election, Google searches for “how to leave the US” spiked more than 1,000 percent in a single week. The US passport, which ranked first globally on the Global Passport Index in 2021, had fallen to 14th by 2025. Ireland welcomed 10,000 Americans in 2025, which is double the number from 2024. Americans are now buying apartments in Dublin’s Grand Canal Dock district at a rate that real estate agents estimate puts one US born resident in every fifteen homes, a share that exceeds the percentage of Americans born in Ireland during the 19th century influx following the Famine. In Lisbon, new arrivals report hearing more English than Portuguese in some neighborhoods.

This is not a fringe movement. It is a broad-based, accelerating, and cross-demographic recalibration of where people believe their lives can be built.

Why people are leaving — and why the official reasons only go so far !!!

Ask researchers and relocation firms what is driving the exodus and you get a consistent list: healthcare costs, housing affordability, political polarization, and the normalization of remote work. All four are real.

None of them tells the full story.

Healthcare is the most quantifiable driver. The United States spends more per capita on healthcare than any other developed nation and produces worse outcomes across most key metrics. Medical debt is a leading cause of personal bankruptcy — a distinction the US shares with no other high-income country. For diaspora families whose parents arrived with nothing and spent decades building financial stability, the prospect of that stability being wiped out by a single health event is not abstract. It is the nightmare at the center of every financial plan.

But talk to the people actually leaving and you hear something the data cannot fully capture: a cumulative weight. Not one rupture but a series of them. A creeping recognition — described by multiple people in interviews across press accounts from late 2025 and early 2026 — that “the issues we were worried about weren’t temporary.” That the political polarization is structural, not cyclical. That the institutions which were supposed to correct course have demonstrated, repeatedly, that they will not.

For the diaspora professional — and this is the part that does not appear in the relocation industry’s marketing materials — there is also the reckoning with conditional belonging. The unspoken contract was always conditional. You were welcome here on terms. The terms included achievement, assimilation, quietness, and the performance of gratitude. When the political environment makes clear that the conditionality was always the point — that belonging was never the destination, only the carrot — the calculation about where to build a life shifts fundamentally.

Where the dream has moved

The top destinations by growth in American visa applications and residency permits tell a story about what people are actually looking for when they leave.

Portugal leads by most measures. US family visa applications tripled since 2024. The D7 and D8 visas offer accessible pathways for remote workers and retirees. A five-year path to citizenship. EU Schengen access. Healthcare ranked among Europe’s most accessible. A cost of living that, in cities outside Lisbon, remains a fraction of major US metros. Portugal also ranked first on the Global Retirement Index.

Ireland draws a specific demographic: educated, English-speaking, with some combination of ancestry-based visa eligibility and proximity to the broader European job market. In 2024, 31,825 Americans secured Irish passports; an estimated 40,000 did so in 2025. More Americans moved to Germany in 2024 than Germans moved to America. Spain, France, Costa Rica, and Thailand each capture different profiles — Spain and France for the EU access and cultural depth, Costa Rica for affordability and universal healthcare without European complexity, Thailand for the cost arbitrage available to remote workers with dollar-denominated incomes.

The common thread across these destinations is not lifestyle. It is security.

A predictable legal system. Healthcare that does not carry existential financial risk. Political stability that, whatever its imperfections, does not produce daily anxious scanning of the news cycle. And — for families with children — the ability to make a multi-generational plan. A second passport is not just personal mobility. It is an inheritance: the option your children will have to live and work across borders that your citizenship alone cannot give them.

What this means for a community that came here to stay. This is the part of the conversation that the emigration data does not capture, because it does not know how to count it.

The diaspora professionals now seriously weighing departure are, in many cases, the same people whose parents came to America specifically because leaving was not a choice they had. The 1947 Partition. The partition of Bangladesh. The violence that cleared entire communities from places they had lived for generations. America was not just an opportunity. It was the place you came when the alternative was worse, when home was no longer safe, when the line had been drawn and you were on the wrong side of it.

To now reassess that choice — to look at this country and begin calculating whether it still represents the safest bet — is not a light decision. It carries grief. Complicated grief: for the country you believed in, for the promise that organized your family’s entire migration story, for the version of America that the mythology described and that may never have fully existed in the way you understood it to.

But it is also, for a generation that watched their parents absorb enormous costs in exchange for conditional acceptance, an act of clarity. The mythology was useful. It moved people across oceans and through generations of sacrifice. It no longer fully describes the situation on the ground. People are updating their priors. That is not disloyalty. That is the thinking that the mythology always claimed to reward.

What it signals systemically

The communities most likely to leave are, by profile, the communities most critical to sustained American competitiveness: college-educated professionals aged 25 to 45, concentrated in technology, healthcare, finance, and the creative industries. The same communities that, in prior decades, came to the United States because it was the best place in the world to build the kind of career and life they wanted to build.

When that calculus shifts — when France, Germany, Portugal, and Ireland are credible alternatives rather than sacrifices — something structural has changed. Not just about American attractiveness, but about the global distribution of opportunity. The dream has not ended. It has become portable. And in a world where talent can route around political environments the way capital routes around taxes, the country that made itself inhospitable to the people most capable of going elsewhere will find that they go.

The question for policymakers — and for the institutions that shaped the environment producing this exodus — is whether anyone in the relevant rooms is paying attention. The renunciation queue is 30,000 people deep. The passport ranking has fallen thirteen places in four years. Net migration turned negative for the first time since 1935.

These are not feelings. They are signals. The question is whether the people who could change the conditions are interested in reading them.

In closing, I am writing this in the middle of my own version of this calculation. The Ireland move is not hypothetical for me — I’m planning my move while family has already taken the leap of faith. And the thinking that brought me here is not despair about America. It is a clearer-eyed assessment of where, in 2026, I can build the kind of life and work I want to build. That assessment includes this country. It also includes other countries. The fact that the comparison is no longer automatic — that America is now one option among several rather than the obvious answer — is itself the story.

The Diaspora Dispatch exists because this community’s experience of these questions is particular. We did not arrive here with the assumption of permanent belonging. We arrived with a conditional offer. The offer is being renegotiated. We are allowed to negotiate back.

The Thinker’s Dilemma is a newsletter and podcast at the intersection of technology, culture, diaspora experience, and systems thinking. Subscribe for new essays weekly. Share with someone navigating the same question.

Thanks for reading! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Get more from Thinkers Dilemma in the Substack app
Available for iOS and Android

items.title: The Housing Crisis Is a Policy Choice
items.author: Thinkers Dilemma
items.description: Where is America heading; Homes or habitats?
items.content:
Here is the math.

In 2026, the median home price in roughly 80 percent of U.S. metropolitan areas is expected to exceed five times the median household income. This is the historical threshold for affordability. The ratio at which a household can reasonably service a mortgage while maintaining other financial obligations is three. Not five. Three.

That gap was not produced by weather. It was not produced by population growth, immigrant waves, or generational laziness. It was produced by zoning votes, tax codes, capital flows, and the deliberate non-construction of housing for forty years. There is no “natural” housing market. There are only the decisions a society makes about land, density, and capital. The crisis is the policy. Calling it a “crisis” obscures the fact that someone chose it.

The Architecture of Scarcity

To understand why housing is expensive, we must disaggregate the real reasons from the stories we tell ourselves about them.

The primary driver is constrained supply. In most U.S. cities, zoning bans apartments and multi-family housing on 75 percent or more of urban residential land. These are not obscure regulations; they are the single most powerful determinant of housing cost in America. They were written to produce exactly the outcome they have produced: restricted supply, rising prices, and the exclusion of lower-income residents from high-opportunity neighborhoods.

This supply constraint is compounded by the “entitlement” process. Entitlement timelines of 18 to 36 months are standard in most major metros, disproportionately affecting small developers who cannot carry the cost of land through years of permitting uncertainty. This concentrates supply in the hands of large, well-capitalized firms, which have the least incentive to build affordable units. When you add construction cost inflation, skilled labor shortages, and the heavy burden of impact fees and design review requirements, the barrier to entry becomes insurmountable for anyone not building luxury products.

While we have been restricting supply, we have been simultaneously subsidizing demand. The mortgage interest deduction is a tax expenditure of roughly $30 billion per year that flows overwhelmingly to higher income homeowners. Furthermore, the era of ultra low interest rates from 2009 to 2022 poured liquidity into the market that was absorbed almost entirely by prices, not production. We subsidized demand for forty years while actively strangling supply.

Finally, we must account for the financialization of the home. Since 2012, institutional ownership of single family rentals has grown dramatically, with large investors purchasing hundreds of thousands of homes in markets where first time buyers must compete against firms with access to nearly limitless, cheap capital. Housing has been transformed: it functions less as shelter and more as a high yield asset class.

The Wealth Transfer Mechanism

Zoning is the load bearing wall of this structure, and its history is less innocent than the word suggests.

Modern land use zoning emerged in the early twentieth century with explicit racial and class purposes. The Supreme Court upheld single-use zoning in Euclid v. Ambler Realty Co. in 1926, providing the legal foundation for municipalities to dictate what could be built and where. Redlining mapped racial exclusion onto physical space, and exclusionary single family designations were designed, openly at the time, to keep out renters. This also meant keeping out the populations associated with “them renters”.

While the legal language became race neutral after the Fair Housing Act of 1968, the spatial outcomes were preserved. Today, single family zoning protects the asset values of existing homeowners by restricting supply. It is, by dollar volume, the most successful wealth transfer mechanism in American policy history.

This creates a powerful “coalition for scarcity.” Existing homeowners benefit because their largest asset appreciates faster than wages. Local governments benefit because the property tax base grows without requiring massive service expansion. Established developers benefit because high barriers to entry protect them from competition.

On the other side: renters, young workers, immigrants, and the unhoused pay the bill. This is a massive, quiet, intergenerational wealth transfer. Every down zoning and every parking mandate that kills a project converts a younger person’s future shelter into an older person’s present net worth. The coalition for scarcity is concentrated, well organized, and votes reliably in the local elections where zoning is decided. The coalition for abundance is diffuse and shows up rarely. This is not a failure of democracy; it is democracy working exactly as designed.

Listen to the discussion on the topic instead.

The International Alternative

The international comparison proves that this scarcity is not inevitable; it is a choice.

Tokyo operates under a nationally pre-empted zoning system that permits approximately 800,000 housing starts per year. Because local authorities cannot restrict construction below the national standard, supply responds to demand, and real housing prices have remained essentially stable for twenty five years.

Vienna utilizes a social housing system that covers approximately 60 percent of the city’s residents, with rents running at roughly 25 percent of comparable U.S. city rents. This functions as a permanent affordability anchor that disciplines the private market.

Singapore’s Housing and Development Board (HDB) houses 80 percent of citizens through publicly constructed apartments sold on 99 year leases. By acting as the primary developer, lender, and regulator, the government eliminates the misaligned incentives that plague the U.S. system.

None of these models require utopia.

They simply require the political will to override incumbent homeowner preferences in the interest of the broader population.

The Breaking of the Settlement

For most of the past half century, housing reform was the issue everyone agreed on in theory and no one could move on in practice. But the politics are finally breaking.

The most consequential development is the migration of housing policy from the city council, where incumbents protect their property values, up to the state legislature, where the math is different. When the decision making body represents an entire state, the renters and the priced out finally outnumber the defenders of the cul-de-sac.

We are seeing a wave of state-level preemption: Oregon, California, and Minnesota have already begun overriding local single-family zoning and legalizing accessory dwelling units by right. A new, cross-ideological “YIMBY” (Yes In My Backyard) movement has emerged. A coalition of market-friendly conservatives who hate regulatory thickets and progressives who view housing as a fundamental justice issue.

Fixing the crisis is not technically difficult; it is politically explosive. The “honest list” of solutions includes:

Statewide preemption of local zoning to prevent municipalities from blocking regional housing needs.

By-right approval for housing that meets objective density thresholds.

Tax reform that decouples property values from local school funding to break the “not in my school district” feedback loop.

Limits on institutional single-family rental ownership to prevent the conversion of the homeownership market into an extraction market.

The technical problems were solved decades ago. The political problem remains: incumbent owners voting their interest, and the rest of us paying for it.

The housing crisis is what happens when the people who benefit from scarcity have political power and the people who pay for it do not. That is not a market outcome. It is a settlement. And settlements can be renegotiated when the cost of the current one becomes intolerable to enough people. We are arriving at that point. The question is whether we will recognize it as a choice and act accordingly, or continue to call it a “crisis” and wait for a market that was designed to fail.

Sources:

Congressional Budget Office, “The Distribution of Major Tax Expenditures,” (cbo.gov).

Urban Institute, Research on Institutional Investor and Single-Family Rental Trends.

Financial Times / Journal of Urban Economics, Comparative Analysis of Tokyo’s Zoning Stability.

City of Vienna, Vienna Housing Report.

Housing and Development Board (Singapore), Annual Reports on Public Housing.

Thanks for reading! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Available for iOS and Android

items.title: Tech Ethics in the Age of AI
items.author: Thinkers Dilemma
items.description: When innovation moves faster than regulation - who pays the price?
items.content:
Every major technology company in the world is currently racing to deploy artificial intelligence faster than its competitors. They are doing this with the full knowledge that no comprehensive regulatory framework yet exists to govern what they are deploying.

They know this because they helped ensure it would be so.

This is not a conspiracy. It is a business model.

Speed is the moat. The company that deploys first captures the market. The harms that emerge, the biased hiring decisions, the misidentified faces, the displaced workers, the data extracted without meaningful consent, those are externalities. In economic terms, an externality is a cost that falls on someone who was not party to the transaction. The transaction happens. The cost is real. It is simply paid by someone else.

The question this essay asks is: who is that someone else? And why is it always the same people?

“The ethical void in AI is not an accident of timing. It is a choice about whose discomfort counts as a problem worth solving.”

The gap is the feature

In 2024, the EU AI Act became the world’s first comprehensive legal framework for artificial intelligence, many years after the systems it now governs had already been deployed across European financial institutions, healthcare systems, hiring platforms, and law enforcement agencies. South Korea enacted its AI Framework Act in January 2026. Japan passed its first AI Basic Act in May 2025. The United States, as of this writing, operates a patchwork: California’s AI Safety Act, New York City’s Local Law 144 requiring bias audits for hiring algorithms, and a handful of federal agency guidelines with limited enforcement teeth.

By the time each of these laws arrived, the technology had been in market for years. The gap between deployment and regulation is not a temporary condition while legislators catch up. It is a structural feature that the industry has every incentive to maintain. The longer the gap, the longer the window to entrench market position, train on data that wouldn’t survive regulatory scrutiny, and establish systems that are functionally too embedded to reverse.

We have seen this pattern before. Social media platforms deployed engagement-maximizing algorithms throughout the 2010s while the academic research on their mental health effects, particularly on teenage girls, was already arriving in peer-reviewed journals. The platforms knew. Regulation followed a decade later, after the harm had scaled globally. The cost was paid by a generation of users. The benefit was captured by shareholders.

AI is running the same play, at greater speed, with higher stakes.

Who pays: the bias tax

In 2018, Amazon scrapped an internal AI hiring tool it had been developing for four years. The system had been trained on a decade of the company’s own hiring decisions. The problem: the previous decade of hiring decisions had been made overwhelmingly by and in favor of men. The algorithm learned the pattern. It systematically downgraded resumes that included the word “women’s” as in, women’s chess club, women’s college and penalized graduates of all-women’s colleges. Amazon quietly shut it down. The tool was never deployed publicly, so legally nothing happened. The story became a cautionary tale cited at conferences. The structural conditions that created it remain unchanged across the industry.

The National Institute of Standards and Technology published a landmark facial recognition study finding that the best algorithms misidentify Black and Asian faces between 10 and 100 times more frequently than white faces. This is not a fringe product or a startup’s prototype. These systems are deployed in law enforcement, airport security, workplace access control, and financial verification systems across the United States and globally. The error rate does not fall equally. It falls specifically on the people whose faces were underrepresented in the training data, which is to say, the people who were historically excluded from the rooms where the datasets were assembled.

Predictive policing tools used in dozens of US cities generate patrol recommendations based on historical crime data. Historical crime data reflects historical policing patterns, which reflect decades of racially biased enforcement. Feed biased data into a model and ask it to predict future crime. The model obliges. Communities that were over-policed in the past get flagged for increased surveillance in the present. The algorithm does not introduce a new bias. It industrializes an existing one at scale and gives it the laundered authority of technical objectivity.

The bias tax is paid by those whose faces are wrongly flagged, whose resumes are filtered out, whose neighborhoods are marked as high-risk, while the system producing those decisions is presented as neutral, efficient, and precise. The system carries no risk or liability for it’s mistakes.

Who pays: the displacement cost

The public conversation about AI and jobs focuses disproportionately on knowledge workers.

Will lawyers be replaced? Will coders be automated away?

These are real questions, but they are not where the displacement is landing hardest first.

The workers absorbing AI displacement right now are largely in logistics and warehousing, in customer service, in content moderation, in data annotation. They are workers for whom job loss means immediate income crisis, not a six-month runway to pivot into prompt engineering. They are disproportionately women, immigrants, and workers in the Global South, including the content moderators in Kenya, the Philippines, and Pakistan who reviewed the violent and traumatic material required to train the safety filters that make AI products usable for Western consumer markets.

TIME Magazine’s 2023 investigation documented that Kenyan workers were paid between $1.32 and $2 per hour to review content including descriptions of child sexual abuse, murder, and torture, material required to train ChatGPT’s safety systems. OpenAI’s valuation at the time of publication was approximately $29 billion. The people absorbing the psychological cost of building the product were earning less than $15 a day. The people capturing the financial value were not in the same room, or the same country, or the same conversation about AI ethics.

This is not incidental to the economics of AI. It is the economics of AI.

Who pays: the data extraction

Every time you write an email, post on social media, take a photograph, have a conversation with a voice assistant, or use a navigation app, you generate data. That data has been collected, retained, sold, and in many cases used to train the AI systems that are now being sold back to you as productivity tools.

The transaction was rarely disclosed in terms a non-lawyer would understand. The consent was buried in terms of service that run to tens of thousands of words and change without meaningful notification. The concept of meaningful consent, the kind where a person understands what they are agreeing to and has a genuine choice about it, has been structurally absent from the data economy since its inception.

Ongoing debates about AI training data reveal unresolved issues of consent, labor, and compensation. Artists whose work was scraped to train image-generation models did not agree to be training data. Writers whose published work forms the corpus for language models were not consulted. The people whose faces are in facial recognition databases did not choose to be there.

Digital sovereignty, the principle that individuals and communities should have genuine rights over their own data, its use, and the systems built from it, is not a technical proposal. It is a basic question of whose interests the system is designed to serve. Right now, the answer is unambiguous: it is designed to serve the interests of the companies with the compute power and the capital to deploy first.

The regulation theater problem

Governments worldwide are now legislating AI. By some counts, 50 percent of governments plan to enforce some form of responsible AI regulation by the end of 2026. Global investment in AI ethics initiatives is projected to exceed $10 billion this year. AI ethics committees are being announced. Responsible AI frameworks are being published.

The vocabulary of accountability: transparency, fairness, explainability, has been successfully absorbed into corporate communications.

And yet the same structures that produced algorithmic bias, exploitative data labor, and unaccountable deployment continue largely undisturbed beneath the vocabulary of responsibility.

The risk is that AI ethics becomes what corporate social responsibility became in the previous decade: a reputational tool that absorbs criticism without requiring structural change. You publish the ethics principles. You create the internal committee. You hire the responsible AI team. You continue to deploy the same systems at the same pace to the same markets, with the same distribution of who benefits and who absorbs the cost — while now being able to point to your published commitment to fairness as evidence that you have taken the issue seriously.

The facade of propriety is not limited to governments. It runs through institutions of every kind.

What would it actually mean to get this right

Regulation alone will not solve this, not because regulation is unimportant, but because regulation follows power. The communities bearing the cost of AI systems need to be in the rooms where those systems are designed, audited, and governed, not consulted after deployment, not acknowledged in the ethics section of an annual report, but present at the beginning, with actual decision making authority.

NYC’s Local Law 144 is a start: requiring employers who use automated hiring tools to conduct independent bias audits and disclose that automated decision making is in use. South Korea’s January 2026 framework mandates fairness and non discrimination across AI systems in high impact sectors. These are not solutions. They are the minimum conditions for a conversation that the industry has been postponing for a decade.

The communities most affected by facial recognition misidentification, algorithmic hiring bias, predictive policing, and data extraction without consent are not waiting for the ethics conferences to conclude. They are already living downstream of systems that were deployed without their input. The question is whether the emerging regulatory architecture will serve them, or will serve as cover for the same extraction dressed in new language.

In Closing this essay I want to highlight several things.

In the last essay, I wrote about the facade of propriety, the pattern by which institutions maintain power through the appearance of legitimacy while operating differently behind closed doors. That essay was about governments and borders. This one is about algorithms and markets.

The pattern is the same.

The price of AI is real. It is paid in misidentified faces, filtered resumes, displaced livelihoods, and data extracted from communities who received no meaningful share of the value they created. The people who built these systems know the price exists. In most cases, they have published papers about it. They have discussed it at conferences. They have hired ethicists to think about it.

They have simply decided that the price is acceptable, because they are not the ones paying it.

That is the dilemma worth thinking about.

The Thinker’s Dilemma is a newsletter and podcast for people who refuse comfortable answers. If this resonated, share it with someone ready to think harder.

Subscribe for new essays every week.

Previous essay: The Facade of Propriety

Thanks for reading! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

items.title: The Facade of Propriety
items.author: Thinkers Dilemma
items.description: An introspection of corruption across systems
items.content:
THE LINE THAT DIDN’T EXIST

In the summer of 1947, a British barrister named Cyril Radcliffe arrived in India for the first and only time in his life. He had five weeks. His assignment: divide a subcontinent of 400 million people. He had no trustworthy maps, no fluency in the languages, no working knowledge of where Punjab ended and Bengal began. He had never been to India before this moment. He would never return after.

On August 17th, 1947, merely two days after India declared independence, and three days after Pakistan the Radcliffe Line was announced. By the time the borders appeared on paper, between 200,000 and two million people were already dead. Fourteen million more were displaced in what remains the largest forced migration in recorded human history.

my family was among those fourteen million.

Not a statistic. Real people. Real houses left unlocked with meals still on the table because leaving was supposed to be temporary. Real children who grew up learning that the ground beneath your feet is not yours by right, it is yours only until someone in a distant room decides otherwise.

Radcliffe destroyed his papers when he returned to England. He refused to collect his fee of 40,000 rupees. He told a colleague he had no desire to hear from anyone in India or Pakistan ever again. He knew what he had done.

“The question this essay asks is not whether Radcliffe knew. The question is whether what he did was an error or a template.”

The doctrine (1904)

Forty-three years before Radcliffe drew his line, Theodore Roosevelt stood before the United States Congress and announced something extraordinary. In his annual address of 1904, Roosevelt declared the United States justified in exercising “international police power” to address what he called “chronic wrongdoing” in the Western Hemisphere. The language was the language of civilization, of moral necessity, of a duty to correct impotence.

This was the Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine, and it was not a policy position. It was a founding document.

Within two decades, the United States had used this doctrine to justify military intervention in Cuba, Nicaragua, Haiti, and the Dominican Republic. Each intervention followed the same sequence: manufacture or identify instability, frame military presence as moral duty, extract strategic advantage while calling it development. Then install a local strongman to manage the population, and when that strongman inevitably brutalizes the population to the point of violence as evidence of the region’s inherent dysfunction. This evidence that justified continued involvement.

The dictators this cycle produced; Batista in Cuba, Trujillo in the Dominican Republic, Somoza in Nicaragua, Duvalier in Haiti were not aberrations. They were graduates of a system the United States built, funded, and sustained. When they eventually became problems, the United States expressed appropriate concern. The connection between student and teacher went unexamined in polite company.

This is what I mean by the facade of propriety. Not corruption hidden behind a curtain. The curtain itself, functioning as the system.

The cold war machine (1947–1991)

By 1947, the same year Radcliffe drew his line, the cycle had industrialized.

In 1979, when Soviet forces entered Afghanistan, the Carter administration made a decision that would reverberate for the next half century. The CIA launched Operation Cyclone: a covert program to arm and finance Afghan mujahideen fighters resisting the Soviet occupation. Funding began at $695,000. By 1987, it had reached $630 million per year. Total expenditure across the program exceeded three billion dollars in US taxpayer money.

The decision to route all aid through Pakistan’s ISI, its intelligence service proved consequential in ways that should have been predictable. Of the seven mujahideen factions receiving support, four held hardline Islamist positions. These factions received the majority of the funding. ISI controlled the distribution. Weapons destined for fighters frequently appeared instead on Karachi’s black market, making the city where many extended families and friends have deep connections one of the most violent in the world within a decade.

Gulbuddin Hekmatyar received an estimated $600 million in US support. He would later order the shelling of Kabul, killing approximately 25,000 civilians in 1994. Mullah Mohammad Omar, a former mujahideen commander, founded the Taliban in 1996. The networks built during Operation Cyclone would become the infrastructure through which Al-Qaeda operated.

The United States spent the next twenty years fighting organizations it had spent three billion dollars creating.

I want to sit with that fact. Not as a rhetorical point, but as a documented reality. The architects of Operation Cyclone knew the ideology of who they were funding. They made a strategic calculation that destabilizing the Soviet Union was worth whatever came after. “Whatever came after” included September 11th, two ground wars, and a generation of displacement across South and Central Asia. The families caught in the wreckage were never part of the calculation. They absorbed the costs of a transaction they never consented to.

The modern cycle (2001–present)

Under the Obama administration, the United States conducted 563 drone strikes targeting Pakistan, Yemen, and Somalia ten times more than under Bush. Between 384 and 807 civilians were killed across those countries according to the Bureau of Investigative Journalism.

The Obama administration classified all military-age males in a strike zone as combatants unless posthumous intelligence proved otherwise. Read that again: you were guilty by geography and gender until proven innocent after death. This methodological choice allowed the administration to simultaneously conduct mass civilian killings and report minimal civilian casualties. Both statements were technically accurate. Neither was true.

One of the first CIA drone strikes under Obama hit a funeral in Pakistan. Forty-one civilians. A 2010 Yemen strike killed 55 people, 21 children under ten, 12 women, five of them pregnant. The Stanford/NYU joint study “Living Under Drones” documented communities where children no longer played outside, where gatherings for weddings and funerals had become potential targets, where years of surveillance produced trauma indistinguishable from active war. Three million Pashtuns were displaced from Waziristan, caught between Pakistani military operations, US drones, and insurgents on three sides.

Many people in the Pakistani American diaspora have extended family lives in these communities. The drone campaign was presented to the American public as surgical and precise. The press largely accepted the framing. This is not incidental to the story. This is the machine working correctly.

Then the machine came home.

The 1033 Program allowed the Department of Defense to transfer surplus military equipment to local law enforcement. By 2014, nearly 10,000 jurisdictions had received over seven billion dollars in equipment. These included combat vehicles, armored personnel carriers, assault rifles, and grenade launchers. Ferguson, Missouri in 2014 made the transfer visible: American police in MRAPs and sniper positions, in military kit designed for Kandahar, confronting American civilians on American streets. Obama restricted the program. Trump reversed the restriction immediately upon taking office. The cycle continued.

The media machine

Every section of this essay has a silent character: the journalist who didn’t ask, the editor who didn’t push, the outlet that ran the approved framing and moved on.

In Pakistan, the mechanism is relatively direct. Government PR budgets pay outlets to carry approved messaging. Journalists who deviate find their access revoked, their careers constrained, their safety reduced to a variable. Real reporting on drone strikes, military operations, and displacement is structurally impossible when the institution with the guns controls editorial access.

In the United States, the mechanism is more refined. Access journalism produces the same result with better manners. The administration that classified civilian funerals as combatant gatherings gave selected journalists background briefings. Those journalists produced stories describing drone strikes as “targeted” and “precise.” The New York Times eventually revealed the “military-age male” classification method, a story that, had it run on page one for two weeks, would have ended careers and potentially a program. It received one news cycle and was absorbed.

The currency differs. Pakistan: direct payment. United States: access, exclusives, and career advancement. The transaction is structurally identical. The story power wants told is the story that gets told.

The structure behind every facade

Here is where I need to complicate this for you because this essay is not ultimately about governments.

The facade of propriety is not a feature of nation-states. It is the operating system of every institution that maintains power through the appearance of legitimacy while operating differently behind closed doors. Corporations perform social responsibility while structuring supply chains around exploitation. Educational institutions perform meritocracy while administering legacy admissions and donor preferences. Families perform unity while distributing resources according to hierarchies that no one names aloud.

The mechanism is always the same: a public story and a private operation, a stated value and an actual calculus, a moral language and an extractive logic. The facade does not contradict the system. The facade enables the system. The facade is the system.

My family was displaced by a line drawn by a man who had never seen the land he was dividing, a man working on behalf of an empire that was performing the orderly transfer of power. He drew the line. The empire announced its values. The bodies were counted later.

I am writing this from a particular location in that history. The displacement that began in 1947 shaped everything that followed for a generation. The geopolitical decisions that followed Operation Cyclone, drone warfare, the management of instability as foreign policy landed on communities I am connected to. When I read the casualty reports from Waziristan, I am not reading about strangers.

But I am writing this because the mechanism is not exotic. It is not a story about distant governments doing terrible things to distant people. It is a story about how every structure you inhabit professional, cultural, national operates the same way: a public story and a private calculus, with the distance between them filled by people who never consented to absorb the cost.

En Conclusion

The Thinker’s Dilemma exists because I believe the most dangerous habit a thinking person can develop is comfort with the official story. Not cynicism! Cynicism is lazy and ultimately another form of detachment. What I mean is the harder practice: taking the official account seriously, testing it against evidence, and being willing to say what you find. In public. Even when the thinking is uncomfortable. Even when it leads somewhere you didn’t expect.

Radcliffe drew a line that displaced fourteen million people. He knew. He burned his papers. He refused his fee.

The question for those of us living downstream of such lines is not whether we can be innocent of what came before. We cannot. The question is whether we are willing to examine it.

This is a first step.

The Thinker’s Dilemma is a newsletter and podcast for people who refuse comfortable answers. If this resonated, share it with someone who is ready to think harder. Subscribe for new essays every week.

Thanks for reading! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.
